brief_document.title: Implement durable conversations with event-sourced state
brief_document.purpose: Implements the heavyweight exactly-once strategy from ADR-006: durable conversations whose processing state is event-sourced to haematite. Every state transition is recorded as an event. On crash and restart, the conversation replays its event log to reconstruct state. Re-delivery of an already-processed message is detected from the event history and results in a no-op. Exactly-once is emergent: make your processing resumable and idempotency is free. This complements DUR-004's lightweight dedup contract for complex multi-step consumers.
brief_document.task: Build the durable conversation module. Define the conversation event types (message_received, processing_started, step_completed, processing_finished, error_occurred). Implement event append with optimistic concurrency via expected_seq — concurrent conversation instances are detected by sequence conflict. Implement event log replay that reconstructs the conversation's processing state. Implement duplicate message detection: on re-delivery, check the event log for a matching message_received event and skip if already processed. Scope boundary: this brief handles conversation state and event sourcing. It does not handle crash recovery orchestration (DUR-006) or channel-level persistence (DUR-002).

requirements.title: Define conversation event types
requirements.spec: ConversationEvent enum SHALL include variants: MessageReceived { message_id: String, received_at: u64 }, ProcessingStarted { message_id: String }, StepCompleted { message_id: String, step_index: u32, output: Vec<u8> }, ProcessingFinished { message_id: String }, ErrorOccurred { message_id: String, error: String }. Each variant SHALL carry sufficient context to reconstruct the conversation state on replay. ConversationEvent SHALL implement Debug, Clone, and serialization to/from bytes.
acceptance: ConversationEvent has exactly 5 variants: MessageReceived, ProcessingStarted, StepCompleted, ProcessingFinished, ErrorOccurred.
StepCompleted carries step_index (u32) and output (Vec<u8>).
All variants carry message_id for correlation.
ConversationEvent round-trips through serialize/deserialize.
ConversationEvent implements Debug and Clone.

requirements.title: Append state transitions with optimistic concurrency
requirements.spec: WHEN a conversation transitions state, THE SYSTEM SHALL serialize the ConversationEvent and call DurableStore::append(conversation_id, event_bytes, expected_seq). The conversation SHALL track its current sequence number, advancing it after each successful append. IF append returns SequenceConflict, THE SYSTEM SHALL return DurabilityError::SequenceConflict — this indicates a concurrent conversation instance (split-brain during failover) and only one instance may proceed.
acceptance: Each state transition calls DurableStore::append with the conversation's expected_seq.
A successful append advances the conversation's internal sequence counter.
A SequenceConflict is returned as DurabilityError::SequenceConflict, not retried.
The stream key for append is the conversation_id.
Two concurrent instances appending to the same conversation_id result in one succeeding and one getting SequenceConflict.

requirements.title: Replay event log to reconstruct conversation state
requirements.spec: WHEN a conversation is recovered (restarted after crash), THE SYSTEM SHALL read all events from DurableStore::read_from(conversation_id, 0, limit) and replay them in sequence order to reconstruct ConversationState. ConversationState SHALL track: which messages have been received, which are currently processing, which steps have completed (with outputs), and which messages have finished processing. Replaying the same event log twice SHALL produce identical state (idempotent replay).
acceptance: Replay of [MessageReceived, ProcessingStarted, StepCompleted(0), StepCompleted(1), ProcessingFinished] produces state with message marked as fully processed with 2 step outputs.
Replay of the same log twice produces identical ConversationState.
ConversationState tracks processed_messages as a set of message_ids.
ConversationState tracks step outputs indexed by (message_id, step_index).
The replay sets the conversation's expected_seq to the last event's sequence + 1.

requirements.title: Detect and skip duplicate message re-delivery
requirements.spec: WHEN a message is re-delivered to a durable conversation, THE SYSTEM SHALL check the ConversationState for a ProcessingFinished event with the same message_id. IF found, THE SYSTEM SHALL skip processing entirely (no-op). IF a MessageReceived event exists but ProcessingFinished does not, THE SYSTEM SHALL resume processing from the last completed step rather than restarting from scratch.
acceptance: Re-delivery of a fully processed message (has ProcessingFinished) results in a no-op — no new events appended.
Re-delivery of a partially processed message (has StepCompleted but no ProcessingFinished) resumes from step_index = last_completed + 1.
Re-delivery of a never-seen message (no MessageReceived) proceeds with normal processing.
The duplicate check uses the ConversationState built from replay, not a separate cache.

requirements.title: Define ConversationState with message tracking and step outputs
requirements.spec: ConversationState struct SHALL track the full processing state reconstructable from the event log. It SHALL contain: received_messages (set of message_ids that have MessageReceived events), in_progress (set of message_ids with ProcessingStarted but no terminal event), completed_steps (map from (message_id, step_index) to output bytes), finished_messages (set of message_ids with ProcessingFinished), and errored_messages (map from message_id to error string). ConversationState SHALL implement Debug.
acceptance: ConversationState has fields: received_messages, in_progress, completed_steps, finished_messages, errored_messages.
is_fully_processed(message_id) returns true iff message_id is in finished_messages.
last_completed_step(message_id) returns the highest step_index in completed_steps for that message, or None.
ConversationState::default() returns an empty state with all collections empty.
ConversationState implements Debug.

boundaries: SHALL NOT implement durable channel storage — that is DUR-002.
SHALL NOT implement consumer cursor checkpointing — that is DUR-003.
SHALL NOT implement dedup key caching — that is DUR-004.
SHALL NOT implement crash recovery orchestration — that is DUR-006.
SHALL NOT retry on SequenceConflict — the conflict signals a concurrent instance, not a transient failure.
SHALL NOT implement conversation timeout or expiry — that is a future operations concern.
verification: cargo check compiles the conversation module with no errors.
Test: replay of event log is idempotent (two replays produce equal ConversationState).
Test: re-delivery of processed message appends no new events.
Test: partial processing resumes from last completed step.

adrs.title: Conversations as the fundamental messaging unit, not messages
adrs.decision: The fundamental unit is the conversation — a supervised beamr process owning a structured exchange between parties — over the message. A conversation mediates lifecycle, failure handling, and (optionally) durability. Exchange patterns (request-reply, streaming, saga) are conversation types with different lifecycle rules, not special-case bolt-ons. The rejected alternative is the NATS/Kafka model: messages as the primitive, patterns assembled from messages.

adrs.title: Two exactly-once strategies: lightweight dedup contract and heavyweight durable conversations
adrs.decision: Two complementary strategies available per-channel. Lightweight: three-party dedup contract (producer idempotency key with TTL, bus deduplicates, consumer provides processing receipt). Heavyweight: durable conversations (consumer processing state is event-sourced, re-delivery triggers resume not re-execution — idempotency is emergent from resumable processing). The rejected alternative is a single exactly-once strategy (either too heavy for simple consumers or insufficient for complex ones).

adrs.title: Haematite (prolly tree) as the storage backend, not a general-purpose database
adrs.decision: Haematite is the sole storage backend. Content-addressed prolly tree with WAL buffer gives us branchable state, structural merge, hash-based sync, and actor-per-shard write parallelism — all properties that directly benefit the bus (conversation state branching, multi-node sync, zero-contention writes). The rejected alternative is pluggable backends (splits testing surface, dilutes the branchable-state differentiator, and adds abstraction overhead for a benefit that doesn't exist yet).
adrs.decided_by: Bono + Frodo + Sir Patrick Stewart convergence

checklist.text: Durable conversation appends each state transition as an event via append(conversation_id, event, expected_seq) to haematite.
Event types include: message_received, processing_started, step_completed (with output), processing_finished, and error_occurred.
On restart, conversation actor replays its event log from haematite and reconstructs its processing state.
Re-delivery of an already-processed message is detected by checking the event log and results in a no-op skip.
Concurrent conversation instances are detected via expected_seq conflict on append; only one instance proceeds.

stories.text: As an application developer, I want my durable conversation to resume from the last completed step after a crash so that side effects from completed steps are never re-executed.
As the Aion engine, I want activity processing to use durable conversations so that multi-step activities resume from their last checkpoint on re-delivery rather than re-executing from scratch.

constraints.text: No file over 500 lines. If a module approaches this limit, extract into submodules.
No panics in production code. .unwrap() and .expect() only in tests.
mod.rs contains ONLY pub mod declarations and pub use re-exports. No logic, no type definitions, no function bodies.
All public types implement Debug. All error types implement std::error::Error via thiserror.
Every haematite append call must include an expected_seq for optimistic concurrency. No blind appends.
Consumer cursor checkpoint must use cas (compare-and-swap), never unconditional write. A stale checkpoint must fail, not silently regress.
Durable conversation replay must be idempotent: replaying the same event log twice produces identical state.
Ephemeral channels must not hold a reference to the DurableStore. The type system must prevent accidental persistence calls on ephemeral channels.
Dedup TTL expiry must never delete a receipt that is younger than the configured TTL, even under clock skew up to 1 second.
All durability operations must propagate haematite errors as DurabilityError variants, never swallow or log-and-continue.

resolved_context.intention:
When this cluster is complete, a developer can declare a channel as durable and get message persistence, replay from offset, and crash recovery without changing their publish/subscribe code. A developer building complex processing can choose a durable conversation and get exactly-once semantics that are emergent from resumable processing — not from delivery guarantees and not from application-level idempotency boilerplate.

The durability layer should feel like a channel property, not a separate system. Setting a channel to durable adds persistence; setting it to ephemeral bypasses haematite entirely with zero overhead. The two exactly-once strategies (lightweight dedup contract and heavyweight durable conversations) are complementary tools chosen per-channel, not competing approaches forced on all consumers.

The bus's relationship with haematite is direct and mechanical: append for writes, cas for cursors, read for replay. No ORM, no query builder, no abstraction that hides what haematite is doing. Developers who understand haematite's append/read/cas model understand the durability layer.
